content.problemStatement:
"What do you know about happiness?" ‚Äî Yoda

Chef is happy only if three conditions hold:


Chef finished cooking a delicious meal
Chef got AC for a programming problem with an almost correct code
Chef got a new problem with a sequence of integers
Today, all three conditions are satisfied. Chef would like you to feel his happiness and provide him with a solution for this new problem with a sequence of integers. The problem is as follows.

You are given a sequence A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã. You need to determine if it is possible to choose two indices iii and jjj such that Ai‚âÝAjA_i \neq A_jAi‚ÄãÓÄÝ=Aj‚Äã, but AAiA_{A_i}AAi‚Äã‚Äã = AAjA_{A_j}AAj‚Äã‚Äã. (If it was possible, Chef would be truly happy.)
constraints: 1‚â§Ai‚â§N1 \le A_i \le N1‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§N for each valid iii
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string `"Truly Happy"` if it is possible to choose required indices or `"Poor Chef"` otherwise.

sampleTestCases.output:
Truly Happy
Poor Chef
Poor Chef
Truly Happy
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: Chef is truly happy because AA3=AA1A_{A_3} = A_{A_1}AA3‚Äã‚Äã=AA1‚Äã‚Äã and A3‚âÝA1A_3 \neq A_1A3‚ÄãÓÄÝ=A1‚Äã.
Example case 2: There is no pair of indices which would make Chef truly happy. For instance, AA3=AA4A_{A_3} = A_{A_4}AA3‚Äã‚Äã=AA4‚Äã‚Äã, but A3=A4A_3 = A_4A3‚Äã=A4‚Äã,

hidden.expectedOutput:
Truly Happy
Poor Chef
Poor Chef
Truly Happy
